file_name: Attachment B - Intent to Propose and Non-Disclosure Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall promptly notify State of a breach of any Confidential Information within forty -eight (48) hours of when Recipient discovers such breach. A breach shall be treated as discovered by Recipient as of the first day on which such breach is known, or by exercising reasonable diligence would have been known, to any person, othe r than the person committing the breach, who is an employee, officer, Recipient Representative or other agent of Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall notify State of a potential breach of Confidential Information at its earliest convenience, but no later than ten (10) business days after a preliminary investigation suggests a possible breach. A breach will only be considered 'discovered' after a final determination by Recipient's internal audit team.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires immediate notification (48 hours) upon discovering a breach, while the modified version allows for a delayed notification (10 business days after a preliminary investigation) and redefines 'discovery' to be after an internal audit. This creates a contradiction with Section 9, which requires Recipient to indemnify State for costs associated with complying with notification requirements under Breach Law, because this alteration introduces an uncertainty regarding timely reporting of breaches, which impacts State's compliance with reporting timelines stipulated by Breach Law and introduces an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient shall indemnify and hold State harmless from and against all liability, loss, cost or expense (including attorneys ' fees) which State may sustain or incur by reason of the breach of any agreements, representations, warranties or covenants of or relating to Recipient or any of the Recip ient Representatives contained in this Agreement or by reason of the enforcement by State of any such agreements, representations, warranties or covenants set forth herein. Without limiting the foregoing, in the event of a breach of PHI or PII or similar breach or wrongful disclosure as defined by an applicable law or regulation requiring notification or other remedial action due to the breach or wrongful disclosure of PHI , PII, or other personal or financial information ( "Breach Law") that arose out of or related to Recipient 's or Recipient Representatives ' acts and omissions, Recipient shall indemnify State against all costs and expenses incurred by State that are associated with complying with the notification requirements under the Breach Law. Such indemnification shall include all costs relate d to notifying individuals or any other entity required to be notified by Breach Law, any remediat ion necessitat ed by the b reach, any fines or penalties arising out of the b reach, and any other actions required or that are customary in the industry to be taken pursuant to the Breach Law (including, without limitation, credit or identity monitoring for affected i ndiv iduals).
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the notification timeframe for a breach from 'forty-eight (48) hours' to 'ten (10) business days after a preliminary investigation'. It also redefines when a breach is considered 'discovered' to be only 'after a final determination by Recipient's internal audit team.' This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which requires the Recipient to indemnify State against costs associated with complying with notification requirements under the Breach Law. The delayed notification and redefined discovery impact the State's ability to comply with Breach Law notification timelines, creating a direct conflict and uncertainty, thus a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees to ensure that any Recipient Representative to whom it provides Confidential Information agrees in writing to the same restrictions and conditions that apply through this Agreement to Recipient with respect to Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will make a reasonable effort to ensure that any Recipient Representative to whom it provides Confidential Information is aware of the confidentiality obligations, but a signed agreement is not mandatory unless explicitly requested by State.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates a written agreement from all Recipient Representatives. The modified version makes the agreement optional unless State explicitly requests it. This directly contradicts Section 4's own later requirement for Recipient Representatives to implement safeguards, since Section 4 begins by saying that any Recipient Representative that receives Confidential Information is required to agree in writing to the same conditions.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Recipient agrees to ensure that any Recipient Representative to whom it provides Confidential Information agrees in writing to the same restrictions and conditions that apply through this Agreement to Recipient with respect to Confidential Information. Such written agreement shall also require the Recipient Repres entative to implement reasonable and appropriate administrative, physical, and technical safeguards that reasonably and appropriately protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of the Confidential Information that it receives. Recipient, and not the State, is solely responsible for Recipient Representatives ' uses and disclosures of Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text and the contradicted text both mandate a written agreement from Recipient Representatives. The changed text makes this agreement optional unless explicitly requested by the State. This directly contradicts the requirement for a written agreement, creating a strong in-text contradiction as defined by the explanation.

perturbation.original_text: All information learned or developed pursuant to this Agreement shall be " Confidential Information " as defin ed in this Agreement and shall be the property of State.
perturbation.changed_text: Information learned or developed independently by Recipient, even if related to the subject matter of this Agreement, shall remain the property of Recipient and will not be considered "Confidential Information."
perturbation.explanation: The original states that all information learned is Confidential Information owned by the State. The modified version carves out an exception for independently developed information. This change creates a direct contradiction with Section 2, which states that *all* Confidential Information, regardless of its origin (including jointly developed information), must be protected. The in-text contradiction arises because now it is not clear if independently developed data that is related to the agreement that is in the possession of the Recipient, has to be protected as Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information (a) supplied by any employee, agent, consultant, or independent contractor of State("State Representatives ") to the Recipient or any employee, agent, officer, director, shareholder, independent contractor or representativ e of the Recipient (collectively, the "Recipient Representatives "), (b) obtained by the Recipient or any Recipient Representatives from any documents, meetings or telephone conversations with any State Representatives or from books or records of State , (c) obtained by the Recipient or any Recipient Representatives or in any other manner including through hosting the software evaluation on Recipient 's website, or (d) jointly or individually developed by State and/or Recipient shall be protected and main tained by the Recipient on a confidential basis and the Recipient shall not use any of the Confidential Information for any purposes (other than as permitted by this Agreement). The Recipient shall refrain from directly or indirectly disclosing any of the Confidential Information to any person, firm, fund, or entity, or knowingly making any Confidential Information available to any others for any use (other than as permitted by this Agreement). Failure to mark any of the Confidential Information as 3 confid ential or proprietary shall not affect its status as Confidential Information. In furtherance and not in limitation of the foregoing provisions, the Recipient shall: (a) Restrict disclosure of the Confidential Information only to those of the persons as may b e absolutely necessary; (b) Advise all persons to whom Confidential Information is disclosed of the strict obligations of confidentiality hereunder; and (c) Take such steps to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information as may be taken to protect the Recipient 's own confidential materials, but in no event shall the Recipient use less than a reasonable degree of care. In addition to the foregoing, Recipient agrees to use reasonable and appropriate administrative, physical and technological safeguards to: (i) prevent use or disclosure of the Confidential Information other than as provided for by this Agreement; and (ii) implement administrative, physical, and technical safeguards that reasonably and appropriately protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of Confidential Information . Recipient represents and warrants that it has implemented, and during the term of this Agreement shall maintain, comprehensive written privacy and security policies and procedures and the necessary administrat ive, technical and physical safeguards appropriate to the size and complexity of Recipient 's operations and the nature and scope of its activities.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception to the definition of "Confidential Information" by stating that information developed independently by the recipient is not considered confidential. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that *all* Confidential Information, regardless of its origin (including jointly or individually developed by State and/or Recipient), must be protected and maintained by the Recipient on a confidential basis. This creates a clear contradiction, as the changed text allows the recipient to treat independently developed information differently from other Confidential Information as defined in the contradicted text, leading to uncertainty about the Recipient's obligations.

perturbation.original_text: When requested by State , the Recipient shall: (a) Return all documents, copies of do cuments, computer records and other means of recording or storing Confidential Information in Recipient 's or Recipient Representatives ' possession to State within five (5) calendar days or, at Recipient 's option, destroy all such items; (b) Certify in writing to State that the Recipient has so complied; and (c) Not use or disclose the Confidential Information or transact business in any manner based upon the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: When requested by State, the Recipient shall make reasonable efforts to comply with the return or destruction of Confidential Information, considering business continuity needs. A detailed log of all actions taken regarding the information will be provided to State instead of certification.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates the return or destruction of information within five days, with a certification of compliance. The modified text introduces 'reasonable efforts' and replaces certification with a log. This contradicts the direct requirement in Section 7(c) that Recipient 'not use or disclose' the information after the request, as 'reasonable efforts' to destroy may imply a period where the information is still accessible and potentially disclosable. In-text contradiction in where and when the information is to be disposed of.
perturbation.contradicted_text: When requested by State , the Recipient shall: (a) Return all documents, copies of do cuments, computer records and other means of recording or storing Confidential Information in Recipient 's or Recipient Representatives ' possession to State within five (5) calendar days or, at Recipient 's option, destroy all such items; (b) Certify in writing to State that the Recipient has so complied; and (c) Not use or disclose the Confidential Information or transact business in any manner based upon the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the mandatory 'return or destruction' of confidential information within five days with 'reasonable efforts,' and replaces a 'certification' with a 'detailed log'. This directly contradicts the requirement in the contradicted text that the Recipient 'not use or disclose' the confidential information after the request, as 'reasonable efforts' implies a period where the information might still be accessible. Also, the certification ensures compliance, while a log does not guarantee immediate disposal, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction. This adheres to the definition of an Inconsistency - In Text Contradiction since the deadlines and obligations conflict.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall maintain professional liability insurance/network security as well as the comprehensive general commercial liability insurance, including coverage to protect against any errors or omis sions of the Recipient and Recipient 's Representatives , crime/dishonesty insurance relating to Recipient and Recipient 's Representatives and the preserving of the Confidential Information and workers ' compensation, if required by applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall maintain general commercial liability insurance and workers' compensation, if required by applicable law. Other insurances are optional, and the Recipient will determine, in its sole discretion, the appropriate level of coverage.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates professional liability, network security, and crime/dishonesty insurance. The modified text makes these optional. This creates a contradiction with Section 9, which states the Recipient shall indemnify State from losses resulting from Recipient's acts or omissions, implying that the insurance coverage is critical for covering those potential losses, especially for data breaches and therefore is contradictory, as section 10 used to have language regarding 'the preserving of the Confidential Information' which would provide further support for Section 9 indemnification requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient shall indemnify and hold State harmless from and against all liability, loss, cost or expense (including attorneys ' fees) which State may sustain or incur by reason of the breach of any agreements, representations, warranties or covenants of or relating to Recipient or any of the Recip ient Representatives contained in this Agreement or by reason of the enforcement by State of any such agreements, representations, warranties or covenants set forth herein. Without limiting the foregoing, in the event of a breach of PHI or PII or similar breach or wrongful disclosure as defined by an applicable law or regulation requiring notification or other remedial action due to the breach or wrongful disclosure of PHI , PII, or other personal or financial information ( "Breach Law") that arose out of or related to Recipient 's or Recipient Representatives ' acts and omissions, Recipient shall indemnify State against all costs and expenses incurred by State that are associated with complying with the notification requirements under the Breach Law. Such indemnification shall include all costs relate d to notifying individuals or any other entity required to be notified by Breach Law, any remediat ion necessitat ed by the b reach, any fines or penalties arising out of the b reach, and any other actions required or that are customary in the industry to be taken pursuant to the Breach Law (including, without limitation, credit or identity monitoring for affected i ndiv iduals).
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated specific insurance types (professional liability, network security, crime/dishonesty). The changed text makes these optional. The contradicted text requires the Recipient to indemnify State from losses resulting from Recipient's acts or omissions, including breaches of PHI/PII. Making the specified insurances optional directly contradicts the indemnification requirement, because it removes the guarantee that Recipient will have sufficient financial means to cover these potential losses, leading to uncertainty about the enforceability of the indemnification clause if the Recipient chooses not to carry the previously mandatory insurances.
